primary.question: What does the term 'Capped Mark' refer to in the University of Essex Undergraduate Rules of Assessment?
primary.expected_answer: A capped mark is a maximum mark that can be awarded for an assessment, and if achieved, no further marks can be gained from re-assessments or supplementary examinations.
primary.actual_answer:
The term 'Capped Mark' refers to a maximum mark that can be achieved for reassessment, which is 40 for modules at Levels 6 and below. For modules at Level 7 and above, the capped mark is 50.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/foundation-year/roa-ug-foundation-year-rules.pdf]
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/phais---yufe-full-year.pdf]

_Note: this answer is based primarily on "roa-ug-foundation-year-rules.pdf". Your question could also relate to other documents (rules often differ by programme, department, or academic year) - tell me which programme or year you mean if this isn't the right one._

retrieval.retrieval_query: What does the term 'Capped Mark' refer to in the University of Essex Undergraduate Rules of Assessment?

judge.justification: The answer provides specific capped marks for reassessments but misses the broader definition of a capped mark as a maximum limit that applies beyond just reassessments.

follow_up.question: Can the capped mark be exceeded by a student's performance in any circumstances?
follow_up.expected_answer: No, the capped mark is the highest mark that can be achieved and cannot be exceeded through re-assessment or supplementary examination.
follow_up.actual_answer:
No, the capped mark cannot be exceeded by a student's performance under normal circumstances. The module aggregate is capped at the pre-resubmission aggregate if the pre-resubmission aggregate was above the pass mark. If the pre-resubmission aggregate was below the pass mark, the resubmission can improve the mark up to the pass mark only.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/msc-ot_25.pdf]
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/roa-ug-ehs-year-1-variations.pdf]
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/specific-courses/roa-ug-ehs-hons-stage-two-final-rules.pdf]

_Note: this answer is based primarily on "pg_dip_periodontology_25.pdf". Your question could also relate to other documents (rules often differ by programme, department, or academic year) - tell me which programme or year you mean if this isn't the right one._

primary.question: What does the University of Essex Rules of Assessment cover regarding the final award?
primary.expected_answer: The Rules of Assessment describe the criteria for achieving the final award, which includes whether you have passed all modules and met the requirements to receive a specific classification or exit award.
primary.actual_answer:
The Rules of Assessment describe the criteria for achieving the final award, including whether you have passed all required modules and met the progression requirements to complete your course successfully.

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but focuses more on progression requirements than the criteria for achieving a final classification or exit award.

follow_up.question: Can you explain what types of classifications are available under the Rules of Assessment?
follow_up.expected_answer: Under the Rules of Assessment, students can receive different classifications based on their performance, which may include first-class honors, second-class upper division, second-class lower division, third-class honors, and pass or fail.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The Rules of Assessment for University of Essex undergraduate programs describe several award classifications, including:

- **Honours Degrees**: These can be classified as First Class Honours, Upper Second Class Honours (2:1), Lower Second Class Honours (2:2), and Third Class Honours.
- **Diplomas of Higher Education** and **Certificates of Continuing Education**: Specific classification criteria are provided for these awards.

For detailed information on the exact criteria for each classification, you should refer to the relevant sections in the Rules of Assessment documents specific to your course or program. The rules outline how final grades are determined based on overall performance across all modules.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-1-variations.pdf]

primary.question: What is the requirement for passing Year One in the three-year honors degree program according to the rules?
primary.expected_answer: Students must pass all modules taken in year one.
primary.actual_answer:
To pass Year One in the three-year honors degree program, you must:
- Pass all modules and achieve 120 credits in the year.
- Achieve a minimum Year Mark of 50 on all courses.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf] (academic_year: 2025-26)
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/4-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-4yr-year-3-rules.pdf] (academic_year: 2023-24)

retrieval.retrieval_query: What is the requirement for passing Year One in the three-year honors degree program according to the rules?

judge.justification: The assistant provides additional requirements not mentioned in the reference answer and includes an irrelevant source document.

follow_up.question: Are there any specific conditions or requirements for a student who fails a module in Year One?
follow_up.expected_answer: Students will have the opportunity to resit failed modules at the end of the year and during the resit assessment period.
follow_up.actual_answer:
For students who fail a module in Year One, the specific conditions and requirements are as follows:

- You can fail a maximum of 30 non-core credits across your degree.
- If you fail 30 non-core credits in Year One, you cannot fail any more credits in your degree (condonement).
- After compulsory reassessment failure, up to 30 non-core credits may be condoned if you have failed 30 non-core credits in Year One.
- If you have failed 15 non-core credits in Year One and pass 120 credits in the year, or fail 15 non-core credits and pass 105 credits from Year One, you can proceed to the next year.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/integrated-masters/roa-ug-mlang-year-1.pdf] (academic_year: 2025-26)
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/3-year-honours-degrees/roa-ug-3yr-year-1-rules.pdf] (academic_year: 2025-26)

follow_up.question: Are there any conditions under which a student can still be awarded the certificate if they do not meet the minimum credit requirement at Level 6?
follow_up.expected_answer: No, to be awarded a pass in the Graduate Certificate, a student should obtain a minimum of 45 credits at Level 6 and the remaining credits at Level 5 or above.
follow_up.actual_answer:
According to the provided context, for the Graduate Certificate program, students must obtain a minimum of 45 credits at Level 6 and the remaining credits at Level 5 or above. However, there are some conditions under which a student can still be awarded the certificate even if they do not meet this exact requirement:

- In the [ug_grad-dip-year-2.pdf](https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/graduate-qualifications/ug_grad-dip-year-2.pdf) document for 2024-2025, it states that a student should "Attempt 60 credits of which at least 45 must be passed, with the remaining credits condoned."

This means that if a student passes at least 45 out of the required 60 credits and has the remaining credits condoned, they can still be awarded the Graduate Certificate.

Sources:

primary.question: What are the conditions for a student to proceed to the final year if they have passed fewer than 120 credits in Year One?
primary.expected_answer: A student may be allowed to proceed to the Final Year, following an attempt at reassessment, having passed a minimum of 105 credits, provided they have passed all the core modules for the Year.
primary.actual_answer:
Based on the provided context, there isn't a specific condition mentioned for proceeding directly to the final year with fewer than 120 credits passed in Year One. The rules generally require passing more credits to proceed to subsequent years, but they do allow for condonement of up to 30 non-core credits if certain conditions are met.

For example:
- If a student has failed 30 credits in Year One and passes 90 credits, they might be allowed to proceed to the final year under specific circumstances (as seen in some documents).
- Alternatively, failing fewer than 120 credits could mean condonement is applicable if certain criteria are met.

However, direct progression without passing a minimum number of credits like 120 is not explicitly stated. For precise conditions, you should refer to the detailed rules for your specific degree program and academic year.

follow_up.question: How is the overall weighted average calculated for the MA Social Work programme?
follow_up.expected_answer: For the purposes of these rules, taught module averages, the overall weighted average for the taught modules and the mark for the dissertation or equivalent will be rounded to the nearest whole number – i.e. 49.5 will count as 50, 49.4 as 49.
follow_up.actual_answer:
The overall weighted average for the MA Social Work programme is calculated based on the credits obtained, with specific criteria for different awards:

- **Pass with Merit**: Requires an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more across 120 PG Diploma credits.
- **Pass with Distinction**: Requires an overall weighted average mark of 70 or more across 135 PG Diploma credits.

Credits are rounded to the nearest whole number, and placements are marked on a pass/fail basis. The pass mark for all modules is 50, and failure with a mark of 40 may be condoned under certain conditions.

Sources:
- [ma_social_work_25.pdf](https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/ma_social_work_25.pdf)

retrieval.retrieval_query: How is the overall weighted average calculated for the MA Social Work programme?

judge.justification: The answer provides some relevant information but lacks the specific detail about rounding grades to the nearest whole number as mentioned in the reference.

primary.question: What are the minimum credit requirements and criteria for passing, gaining a Merit, or achieving Distinction in the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program at the University of Essex?
primary.expected_answer: To pass, students must obtain 180 credits at Master's level and pass all assessment components in core modules. To gain a Merit, they should have an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more. For Distinction, they need to achieve an overall weighted average mark of 70 or more.
primary.actual_answer:
For the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program:

- **Pass**: Obtain 150 credits at Masters Level (Level 7), pass all assessment components except SE760: Research Project, and achieve an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more.
- **Merit**: In addition to passing the requirements for a Postgraduate Diploma, have an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more.
- **Distinction**: In addition to passing the requirements for a Postgraduate Diploma, have an overall weighted average mark of 70 or more.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/msc-physiotherapy-25.pdf]

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the minimum credit requirements and criteria for passing, gaining a Merit, or achieving Distinction in the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program at the University of Essex?

judge.justification: The assistant incorrectly states the credit requirement as 150 instead of 180 and misrepresents the criteria for passing and achieving higher classifications.

follow_up.question: What happens if a student fails to meet the minimum credit requirements for the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program but still wants to complete their studies?
follow_up.expected_answer: Where a student is unable to complete their studies or does not achieve the required credits to be awarded a Masters, they may be considered for an exit award of either a Postgraduate Diploma or a Postgraduate Certificate.
follow_up.actual_answer:
If a student is unable to complete the full MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program or does not achieve the required credits, they may be eligible for an exit award of either a Postgraduate Diploma in Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) or a Postgraduate Certificate. The specific requirements are:

- **Postgraduate Diploma in Physiotherapy (Pre-registration)**: 
  - Obtain 150 credits at Masters Level (Level 7).
  - Pass all assessment components except SE760: Research Project.
  
- **Postgraduate Certificate**: 
  - Attempt 60 taught module credits at Masters Level (Level 7) of which 40 must be passed, with the remaining credits condoned.

Sources:
- [https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/msc-physiotherapy-25.pdf]

retrieval.retrieval_query: What happens if a student fails to meet the minimum credit requirements for the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program but still wants to complete their studies?

judge.justification: The answer is mostly correct but includes specific requirements not present in the reference answer.

The pass mark for all modules of the Masters degree is 50. Credits are only awarded if the module is passed.

follow_up.question: What happens if a student fails a core module and how does it affect their progression to Year Two?
follow_up.expected_answer: Students will still be permitted to progress to Year Two if they have failed a core module, but only if they can still be offered further reassessment attempts or are eligible for an exit award of a Postgraduate Diploma or a Postgraduate Certificate.
follow_up.actual_answer:
If a student fails a core module, they will not be permitted to proceed to Year Two unless they are eligible for an exit award of a Postgraduate Diploma or a Postgraduate Certificate. The scenarios where students can still progress despite failing a core module include:

- Having passed all other core modules.
- Still having enough new credits to study in Year Two to aim for an award higher than the one for which they are already eligible at the end of Year One.

In these cases, the student will be given the opportunity to undertake reassessment. If further reassessment is not permitted due to extenuating circumstances that have been accepted by the Board of Examiners, students may still proceed with their studies but will need to meet specific criteria set out by the Board.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/pgt/2025-26/department-specific/msc-human-resource-management-25.pdf]

primary.question: What are the learning outcomes related to assessment as per the rules of assessment document?
primary.expected_answer: Learning outcomes identify what a successful student is expected to know and understand, together with the skills that they should be able to demonstrate on successful completion of the award. primary.actual_answer:
Learning outcomes are what you are expected to know and understand, together with the skills that you should be able to demonstrate on successful completion of a module or award. primary.question: What are the general principles for reassessment according to the rules of assessment document?
primary.expected_answer: The general principles for reassessment apply throughout all years, including Year One, Two, Three, and the Final Year, but specific details vary by year.
primary.actual_answer:
According to the provided documents, the general principles for reassessment include:

- You need to take reassessment if you have failed any module(s) after completing all your assessments but have a Year Mark of 20 or above.
- The Board of Examiners can permit up to two further attempts at any module, with marks capped at 40 unless extenuating circumstances are substantiated.
- You will be offered all the reassessment opportunities available.
- If you attempt reassessment and your mark is lower than a previous attempt, the higher mark will be used to determine progression.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/chula---original-course-structure.pdf]

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the general principles for reassessment according to the rules of assessment document?

judge.justification: The answer provides detailed information but omits mentioning that these principles apply throughout all years as stated in the reference.

follow_up.question: What are the compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One according to the document?
follow_up.expected_answer: Compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One are provided after students have completed their assessments and before the resit assessment period.
follow_up.actual_answer:
According to the document "roa-ug-aegean-omiros-3yr-standard-year-1.pdf" for the academic year 2025-26, if you do not meet the requirements to progress but achieve a Year Mark of 20 or above at the end of Year One, you will be offered reassessment. Specifically:

- If you have failed up to 30 credits, you can:
  - Remain in Year One and take reassessment.
  - Progress to Year Two trailing the failed credits and take a further reassessment attempt before the end of the Autumn Term of Year Two.

If you do not pass the final reassessment attempt (third attempt), you will be required to withdraw from the College.

Sources:
- [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/directories/academic-section/rules-of-assessment/ug/current/partner-institutions/roa-ug-aegean-omiros-3yr-standard-year-1.pdf]

retrieval.retrieval_query: What are the compulsory reassessment opportunities at the end of Year One according to the document?

judge.justification: The answer provides additional details not asked for and includes a future date that does not match the reference.
